title: Add new exit codes to rm & rmi for running containers & dependencies
body:
This enables programs and scripts wrapping the podman command to handle
'podman rm' and 'podman rmi' failures caused by paused or running
containers or due to images having other child images or dependent
containers. These errors are common enough that it makes sense to have
a more machine readable way of detecting them than parsing the standard
error output.

Signed-off-by: Ondrej Zoder <ozoder@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Daniel J Walsh <dwalsh@redhat.com>

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *rhatdan* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

I like the idea of different error codes as @vrothberg suggests, but am concerned about making sure we dn't overlap them?  Perhaps the way to assure that is to have a list of all the codes in a central location?  Looks like we started to do so in the main podman man page, perhaps that's the spot?
@TomSweeneyRedHat, I agree, https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/docs/podman.1.md#exit-status seems like a good central location.
What's going on with the test failures here?
I don't know why these tests are failing, they pass locally rebased and re-pushed, we shall see.
@rhatdan, can you rebase to check if it was a CI flake?
@vrothberg Done, will see what CI says.
@rhatdan, doesn't look like a flake. https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/test/e2e/pause_test.go#L129, for instance, should still exit with `125`.
@vrothberg No it shouldn't.   It should exit with 2, meaning the container is in an invalid state.  Running this test locally passes.

Ok I figured it out, we were not returning the correct error from varlink and then not interpreting it correctly in the client.  Now we will send back the proper error message type from the server and the client can exit with the correct exit code.
@mheon @vrothberg @TomSweeneyRedHat @baude This is finally ready to merge.
